title: Linked List Cycle
description: Given head, the head of a linked list, determine if the linked list has a cycle in it. There is a cycle in a linked list if there is some node in the list that can be reached again by continuously following the next pointer. Internally, pos is used to denote the index of the node that tail's next pointer is connected to. Note that pos is not passed as a parameter. Return true if there is a cycle in the linked list. Otherwise, return false.
hints: Use two pointers, one moving one step at a time and the other moving two steps at a time.
If there is a cycle, the fast pointer will eventually meet the slow pointer.
Think of it as two runners on a circular track running at different speeds.

title: Middle of the Linked List
description: Given the head of a singly linked list, return the middle node of the linked list. If there are two middle nodes, return the second middle node.
hints: Use a slow pointer that moves one step at a time and a fast pointer that moves two steps.
When the fast pointer reaches the end, the slow pointer will be at the middle.
For even-length lists, this approach naturally gives the second middle node.

solutions.python:
def middleNode(head):
    slow = head
    fast = head
    while fast and fast.next:
        slow = slow.next
        fast = fast.next.next
    return slow

title: Happy Number
description: Write an algorithm to determine if a number n is happy. A happy number is a number defined by the following process: Starting with any positive integer, replace the number by the sum of the squares of its digits. Repeat the process until the number equals 1 (where it will stay), or it loops endlessly in a cycle which does not include 1. Those numbers for which this process ends in 1 are happy. Return true if n is happy, otherwise return false.
hints: Think of the sequence of numbers as a linked list where each number points to the sum of squares of its digits.
Use fast and slow pointers to detect a cycle in this sequence.
If there is a cycle, the number is not happy. If the sequence reaches 1, it is happy.

title: Linked List Cycle II
description: Given the head of a linked list, return the node where the cycle begins. If there is no cycle, return null. There is a cycle in a linked list if there is some node in the list that can be reached again by continuously following the next pointer. Internally, pos is used to denote the index of the node that tail's next pointer is connected to. Note that pos is not passed as a parameter. Do not modify the linked list.
hints: After detecting a cycle with fast and slow pointers, reset one pointer to head.
Move both pointers one step at a time until they meet again.
The meeting point is the start of the cycle.

title: Find the Duplicate Number
description: Given an array of integers nums containing n + 1 integers where each integer is in the range [1, n] inclusive. There is only one repeated number in nums, return this repeated number. You must solve the problem without modifying the array nums and use only constant extra space.
hints: Treat the array as a linked list where index i points to nums[i].
The duplicate number creates a cycle in this linked list.
Use Floyd's cycle detection algorithm to find the start of the cycle.

title: Reorder List
description: You are given the head of a singly linked-list. The list can be represented as: L0 → L1 → … → Ln-1 → Ln. Reorder the list to be on the following form: L0 → Ln → L1 → Ln-1 → L2 → Ln-2 → …. You must not modify the values in the list's nodes. Only nodes themselves may be changed.
hints: Find the middle of the list using fast and slow pointers.
Reverse the second half of the list.
Merge the first half and the reversed second half alternately.

solutions.python:
def reorderList(head):
    if not head or not head.next:
        return
    slow = head
    fast = head
    while fast.next and fast.next.next:
        slow = slow.next
        fast = fast.next.next
    prev = None
    curr = slow.next
    slow.next = None
    while curr:
        nxt = curr.next
        curr.next = prev
        prev = curr
        curr = nxt
    first = head
    second = prev
    while second:
        tmp1 = first.next
        tmp2 = second.next
        first.next = second
        second.next = tmp1
        first = tmp1
        second = tmp2

title: Palindrome Linked List
description: Given the head of a singly linked list, return true if it is a palindrome or false otherwise.
hints: Find the middle of the linked list using fast and slow pointers.
Reverse the second half of the linked list.
Compare the first half with the reversed second half.

solutions.python:
def isPalindrome(head):
    if not head or not head.next:
        return True
    slow = head
    fast = head
    while fast.next and fast.next.next:
        slow = slow.next
        fast = fast.next.next
    prev = None
    curr = slow.next
    while curr:
        nxt = curr.next
        curr.next = prev
        prev = curr
        curr = nxt
    left = head
    right = prev
    while right:
        if left.val != right.val:
            return False
        left = left.next
        right = right.next
    return True

title: Remove Nth Node From End of List
description: Given the head of a linked list, remove the nth node from the end of the list and return its head.
hints: Use two pointers with an n-step gap between them.
Advance the first pointer n steps ahead, then move both pointers together.
When the first pointer reaches the end, the second pointer is at the node to remove.

solutions.python:
def removeNthFromEnd(head, n):
    dummy = ListNode(0)
    dummy.next = head
    fast = dummy
    slow = dummy
    for i in range(n + 1):
        fast = fast.next
    while fast:
        fast = fast.next
        slow = slow.next
    slow.next = slow.next.next
    return dummy.next

title: Merge k Sorted Lists
description: You are given an array of k linked-lists lists, each linked-list is sorted in ascending order. Merge all the linked-lists into one sorted linked-list and return it.
hints: Use a divide and conquer approach, similar to merge sort.
Merge pairs of lists repeatedly until only one list remains.
Alternatively, use a min-heap (priority queue) to always pick the smallest element.
timeComplexity: O(n log k) where n is the total number of nodes

title: Rotate List
description: Given the head of a linked list, rotate the list to the right by k places.
hints: Connect the last node to the first node to form a cycle.
Find the node that will become the new tail using (length - k % length) steps.
Break the cycle at the appropriate point.

solutions.python:
def rotateRight(head, k):
    if not head or not head.next or k == 0:
        return head
    length = 1
    tail = head
    while tail.next:
        tail = tail.next
        length += 1
    k = k % length
    if k == 0:
        return head
    tail.next = head
    steps_to_new_tail = length - k
    new_tail = head
    for _ in range(steps_to_new_tail - 1):
        new_tail = new_tail.next
    new_head = new_tail.next
    new_tail.next = None
    return new_head
